title: Lecture 07.12.2025
transcript: vSE 380 – Mobile Application Development Lecture 2: Declarative UI Lecturer: Gazihan Alankuş All rights reserved, cannot be used in any educational institution other than Izmir University of Economics. Please look at the couple of slides for assignments (marked with TODO) A problem: source of information Declarative Programming • In • Flutter • React/React Native • Jetpack Compose (Android) • Swift UI (iOS) we create the user interface in a style called “declarative programming”. • The kind of programming that most of you are used to is called “imperative programming”. • Android SDK • iOS SDK • What is the difference? What exactly are they? A computer program vs. an electronic circuit Imperative vs. Declarative Imperative Declarative • Giving orders in detail, with explicit instructions • Describing how it should be • Do this, then do that, then do this other thing. • Programmer has full control. • Programmer has full responsibility. • The computer is dumb. • Here is how I would like it to look like. I don’t have control over how exactly you are doing it. • Programmer has limited control. • Programmer has limited responsibility. • The computer is smart. Imperative vs. Declarative Imperative Declarative • Java • C++ • Step-by-step programming • HTML • SQL • Designing a circuit-like system • Procedural languages • Functional languages Is it just the language? • No, it’s the style. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Mt7KnvnNGfk How are React Native and Flutter Declarative? • JavaScript and Dart are procedural languages. Shouldn’t it be imperative? • Answer: there are imperative parts of your code. The code that generates the UI is declarative. Declarative code from React Native and Flutter. Declarative code from React Native and Flutter. • Although the language is suitable for imperative code, the framework forces declarative code. • You create and tie UI elements together. • You do not modify or manage existing UI elements. • There is no such code in either: • txtField.text = “new text” • pnlMain.addChild(txtField) • Instead, there is such code: • new Panel(new TextField(“hello”)); UI Code • React Native • JSX • (short for JavaScript XML) • Similar to HTML • The description for the UI. Creates the UI. • Not actual code • Limited customization • No debugging • Flutter • Widget constructors • Named parameters • child for single child • children for multiple • Actual code! • Fully customizable • Fully debuggable UI is a tree • The UI is a tree structure • Creating a tree is easy • Modifying and maintaining a tree is difficult • This is why they chose declarative • We create and re-create UI elements. We do not manage existing elements. • What? How? • How do you make a text field say “one” first, and then say “two” after you press a button? Imperative Example • How do you make a text field say “one” first, and then say “two” after you press a button? Panel panel = new Panel(); TextField t = new TextField(); panel.add(t); t.text = “one”; Button b = new Button(); panel.add(b); b.addClickHandler(() { t.text = “two”; }); • THIS IS IMPERATIVE CODE. THIS MAKES SENSE. • This is not how React Native or Flutter do it. Declarative Example • How do you make a text field say “one” first, and then say “two” after you press a button? String currentValue = “one”; buildUi() { new Panel( new Text(currentValue), new Button( onClick: () { currentValue = “two”; rebuildUi(); } ) ) } • THIS IS DECLARATIVE CODE. THIS IS COUNTER-INTUITIVE • Why does React Native and Flutter use this pattern? Compare them. Panel panel = new Panel(); TextField t = new TextField(); panel.add(t); t.text = “one”; Button b = new Button(); panel.add(b); b.addClickHandler(() { String currentValue = “one”; buildUi() { new Panel( new Text(currentValue), new Button( onClick: () { currentValue = “two”; rebuildUi(); } ) t.text = “two”; }); ) } The difference • Imperative • Declarative • You create elements • You attach them together • You create elements • You attach them together • You keep a reference to elements • You do not keep a reference to elements • You are not responsible for maintaining elements • You do not give them orders • You create them and forget about them • You are responsible for mainaining them • You give them orders • You recreate them when something changes How can you keep recreating the UI? • Isn’t it inefficient? How can you keep recreating the UI? • Isn’t it inefficient? • NO! People smarter than us have implemented it carefully. • What you are creating is not the real UI element. It’s just a light description of them. • Very light data structure, system is ok with creating and destroying them quickly. • The framework finds the difference between two versions of the tree and modifies the UI elements whose description actually changed. • Carefully crafted framework to give you the ability to “just recreate the UI”. • DO NOT TRY TO OPTIMIZE ANYTHING ABOUT IT!!! • DO NOT CACHE UI ELEMENTS, ETC. KEEP RECREATING THEM. Planning and Creating a Declarative UI • How should you go about planning your UI? • https://reactjs.org/docs/thinking-in-react.html •FilterableProductTable • SearchBar • ProductTable • ProductCategoryRow • ProductRow How does data flow? https://livebook.manning.com/book/react-in-action/chapter-3/112 How does data flow? https://www.slideshare.net/pheadra4/react-46872849?from_action=save How does data flow? https://www.slideshare.net/pheadra4/react-46872849?from_action=save How does data flow? • Unidirectional data flow • Top to bottom • Props/constructor arguments (fixed) • Data being passed down when component is created (yellow). • State (changing) • Temporary data that changes with user input or other events • Triggers a rebuild of subtree. Data is passed down as new version of props. • Bottom to top • Events • Lower level component communicates up https://tkssharma.gitbook.io/react-training/day-01/react-js-3-principles/one-way-data-flow How does data flow? • Unidirectional data flow • Top to bottom • Props/constructor arguments (fixed) • Data being passed down when component is created (yellow). • State (changing) • Temporary data that changes with user input or other events • Triggers a rebuild of subtree. Data is passed down as new version of props. • Bottom to top • Events • Lower level component communicates up https://tkssharma.gitbook.io/react-training/day-01/react-js-3-principles/one-way-data-flow Other approaches • Passing data around can be cumbersome • All intermediate levels need to know about the data being passed to a leaf • Solution: state management • Redux • MobX • Provider • Etc. • WARNING: Learn the basics well before starting these approaches! • Try it with setState first. Write a couple of apps with it first. After you really feel the need, start learning about a state management approach. TODO: Groups • I will create groups in Blackboard • I will randomly assign people who did not have groups • Please check tomorrow and let me know if there is a mistake TODO: Project Ideas • The deadline is next week. • Details are in week 1’s slides

plan.topic: Declarative UI for Mobile Application Development

modules.title: Module 1: Foundations of Declarative UI
modules.goal: Understand the core concepts of declarative programming and its application to user interfaces.

lessons.title: Lesson 1.1: Introduction to Declarative Programming
lessons.objective: Differentiate between imperative and declarative programming paradigms and identify frameworks that utilize them.

activities.prompt: Review slide content on 'Declarative Programming' and the initial 'Imperative vs. Declarative' comparison tables.
activities.expected_outcome: Identify the definitions and key distinctions between the two paradigms.

activities.prompt: Watch the first 10 minutes of the lecture explaining the high-level difference and examples (Flutter, React Native, Jetpack Compose, Swift UI).
activities.expected_outcome: Recognize popular frameworks that use declarative UI and understand the conceptual shift.

activities.prompt: Complete the mini-quiz for this lesson.
activities.expected_outcome: Assess understanding of basic definitions and examples of declarative frameworks.

mini_quiz: Which programming paradigm involves explicitly instructing the computer step-by-step?
Name two mobile UI frameworks that primarily use a declarative programming style.
Is HTML considered an imperative or declarative language, and why?

lessons.title: Lesson 1.2: Deep Dive into Imperative vs. Declarative UI
lessons.objective: Analyze the fundamental differences in control, responsibility, and approach between imperative and declarative UI development, including practical examples.

activities.prompt: Study the detailed 'Imperative vs. Declarative' slides, focusing on the bullet points comparing control, responsibility, and computer intelligence.
activities.expected_outcome: Identify the roles of the programmer and computer in each paradigm and their implications.

activities.prompt: Review the lecture explanation comparing 'Computer program vs. electronic circuit' and the detailed code examples of imperative and declarative UI for updating a text field.
activities.expected_outcome: Understand the analogies and practical code differences between modifying existing UI vs. recreating it.

activities.prompt: Sketch a simple UI component (e.g., a button changing text) and write pseudo-code for both imperative and declarative approaches to demonstrate the interaction.
activities.expected_outcome: Apply the learned concepts to a basic UI scenario and visualize the code structure.

mini_quiz: In an imperative UI approach, who typically has full control and responsibility: the programmer or the computer?
Explain how an electronic circuit serves as an analogy for declarative programming.
What is a key difference in how UI elements are managed (modified vs. recreated) in imperative versus declarative paradigms?

lessons.title: Lesson 1.3: The Tree Structure of User Interfaces
lessons.objective: Explain why UIs are conceptualized as tree structures and how this concept influences the design choice for declarative frameworks.

activities.prompt: Examine the slide titled 'UI is a tree'.
activities.expected_outcome: Visualize how nested UI components form a hierarchical tree structure.

activities.prompt: Listen to the lecture segment discussing UI as a tree structure and the challenges humans face with tree traversal and maintenance.
activities.expected_outcome: Understand the inherent difficulties of managing UI trees imperatively and how declarative frameworks aim to simplify this.

activities.prompt: Complete the mini-quiz for this lesson.
activities.expected_outcome: Assess understanding of the UI tree concept and its relevance to declarative programming.

mini_quiz: Describe how a typical mobile application's UI can be viewed as a tree structure.
According to the lecture, what common programming difficulty is mitigated by adopting a declarative approach to UI trees?

modules.title: Module 2: Declarative UI in Modern Mobile Frameworks
modules.goal: Understand how modern mobile frameworks implement declarative UI and its practical implications for development.

lessons.title: Lesson 2.1: Frameworks' Declarative Implementation
lessons.objective: Describe how frameworks like Flutter and React Native achieve declarative UI despite using procedural languages, and identify their core UI building blocks.

activities.prompt: Read slides on 'How are React Native and Flutter Declarative?' and 'UI Code (React Native/Flutter)'.
activities.expected_outcome: Identify the specific syntax and concepts (e.g., JSX, Widget constructors) that enable declarative UI in these frameworks.

activities.prompt: Review the lecture segment explaining that the *framework* enforces declarative style even with procedural languages, and the specific UI code structures.
activities.expected_outcome: Grasp how procedural languages are adapted to a declarative UI paradigm by the framework.

activities.prompt: Research a simple Flutter Widget or React Native Component and identify how it declares its UI elements without directly modifying them.
activities.expected_outcome: Connect theoretical concepts to practical code examples in a chosen framework.

mini_quiz: Why are Dart (Flutter) and JavaScript (React Native) considered procedural languages, yet their UI development is declarative?
What are the primary UI building blocks in Flutter and React Native, respectively?
Give an example of code you would NOT typically see in a declarative UI framework like Flutter or React Native.

lessons.title: Lesson 2.2: UI Recreation and Optimization
lessons.objective: Explain the concept of recreating the entire UI tree on state changes and understand why this seemingly inefficient approach is performant.

activities.prompt: Review the slides asking 'How can you keep recreating the UI?'.
activities.expected_outcome: Understand the central question regarding the efficiency of declarative UI frameworks.

activities.prompt: Pay close attention to the lecture explanation about the 'light description' of UI elements and the framework's role in finding differences. Note the explicit advice on optimization.
activities.expected_outcome: Grasp the underlying mechanism that makes UI recreation efficient and learn critical best practices regarding optimization.

activities.prompt: Complete the mini-quiz for this lesson.
activities.expected_outcome: Assess understanding of declarative UI rendering mechanisms and optimization guidelines.

mini_quiz: Why is repeatedly recreating UI elements in declarative frameworks not necessarily inefficient?
What is the key advice given regarding optimizing UI element creation in declarative frameworks?

modules.title: Module 3: Data Management in Declarative UIs
modules.goal: Learn how data flows and is managed within declarative UI architectures, including basic and advanced strategies.

lessons.title: Lesson 3.1: Unidirectional Data Flow
lessons.objective: Understand the principles of unidirectional data flow, distinguishing between props/constructor arguments and state, and how components communicate changes.

activities.prompt: Study the 'How does data flow?' slides, specifically the bullet points on unidirectional flow, props, state, and events.
activities.expected_outcome: Identify the core components of unidirectional data flow (props, state, events) and their roles.

activities.prompt: Listen to the lecture's explanation of data flow and the difference between fixed data (props) and changing data (state), and how events propagate.
activities.expected_outcome: Understand how data is passed down and events are communicated up the component tree.

activities.prompt: Analyze the 'FilterableProductTable' example structure (if available via provided links) and identify potential data flow paths for user interactions (e.g., search input, product selection).
activities.expected_outcome: Apply unidirectional data flow concepts to a practical, hierarchical UI example.

mini_quiz: What are 'props' or 'constructor arguments' used for in unidirectional data flow?
How does 'state' differ from 'props' in a declarative UI component?
How do lower-level components typically communicate changes to higher-level components in a unidirectional data flow?

lessons.title: Lesson 3.2: Advanced State Management
lessons.objective: Recognize the need for advanced state management solutions in complex applications and understand the recommended approach to learning them.

activities.prompt: Review the slide 'Other approaches' regarding state management solutions like Redux, MobX, and Provider.
activities.expected_outcome: Identify different advanced state management libraries and understand their purpose.

activities.prompt: Listen to the lecture's caution about when to adopt state management libraries.
activities.expected_outcome: Learn the recommended progression for integrating advanced state management into a project.

activities.prompt: Complete the mini-quiz for this lesson.
activities.expected_outcome: Assess understanding of when and how to approach advanced state management in declarative UIs.

mini_quiz: When should a developer consider adopting an advanced state management solution?
What is the recommended initial approach before delving into complex state management libraries?

emphases.statement: The live lecture is the primary and most important source of information for this course, more so than recordings.
emphases.why: This instruction emphasizes the importance of active engagement and attendance, which directly contributes to better understanding and exam performance, as recordings might be incomplete or secondary sources.
emphases.evidence: The teacher explicitly states that main sources require being present and listening, implying that recordings are supplementary.
emphases.from_transcript_quote: Main sources you need to be here and listening to the lecture.

emphases.statement: Flutter's developer experience, specifically hot reload with state preservation and direct UI-to-code navigation, is extremely valuable for productivity and unmatched.
emphases.why: These features drastically reduce the development feedback loop and simplify debugging complex UI states, making development faster and more enjoyable. Understanding these 'luxuries' highlights practical advantages of the framework.
emphases.evidence: The teacher dedicates a significant portion of the introduction to elaborate on these 'luxuries,' providing a detailed e-commerce app scenario and calling them 'immensely valuable' and 'unmatched.'
emphases.from_transcript_quote: So this is immensely valuable and it reduces the loop that I talked about... But we are living in a luxurious world with Flutter. I told you that developer experience is unmatched pretty much.

emphases.statement: Declarative UI programming fundamentally shifts control and responsibility from the programmer (who gives explicit orders) to the 'smart' computer/framework (which executes based on descriptions).
emphases.why: This is the core philosophical difference. Grasping this paradigm shift is crucial for understanding why certain programming patterns (like UI recreation) are adopted and how declarative frameworks simplify UI development. This distinction is fundamental for conceptual understanding and exam questions.
emphases.evidence: Extensive explanation through analogies (computer program vs. electronic circuit) and explicit comparison tables on slides detailing control, responsibility, and perceived computer intelligence in both paradigms.
emphases.from_transcript_quote: In declarative world, your program has limited control, but has limited responsibility also, right?... And the computer is somewhat smart. You give it these instructions, and then it does some things that you don't want to do, such as treat traverses.
emphases.from_slide_quote: Imperative: Programmer has full control. Programmer has full responsibility. The computer is dumb. Declarative: Programmer has limited control. Programmer has limited responsibility. The computer is smart.

emphases.statement: In declarative UI, developers should consistently recreate UI elements rather than attempting manual optimization or caching, as the framework is intelligently designed to handle this efficiently.
emphases.why: This is a counter-intuitive yet critical principle for effective declarative UI development. Misunderstanding this leads to incorrect optimization attempts, performance issues, and breaking the declarative model. This directly impacts practical development best practices and is highly relevant for exam scenarios.
emphases.evidence: Direct, strong injunctions are given: 'DO NOT TRY TO OPTIMIZE ANYTHING ABOUT IT!!! DO NOT CACHE UI ELEMENTS, ETC. KEEP RECREATING THEM.' The slides also explain the framework's internal diffing mechanism.
emphases.from_transcript_quote: DO NOT TRY TO OPTIMIZE ANYTHING ABOUT IT!!! DO NOT CACHE UI ELEMENTS, ETC. KEEP RECREATING THEM.
emphases.from_slide_quote: NO! People smarter than us have implemented it carefully. ... The framework finds the difference between two versions of the tree and modifies the UI elements whose description actually changed.

emphases.statement: Advanced state management solutions (e.g., Redux, MobX, Provider) should only be adopted after mastering basic state management (`setState`) and experiencing a genuine need for them in complex applications.
emphases.why: This practical advice prevents premature over-engineering, ensures a solid understanding of fundamental data flow, and avoids introducing unnecessary complexity into simpler projects. It’s crucial for making informed architectural decisions in real-world development.
emphases.evidence: Explicit 'WARNING' on the slides is supported by extended verbal explanation, emphasizing trying basic methods first before escalating to complex libraries.
emphases.from_transcript_quote: WARNING: Learn the basics well before starting these approaches! Try it with setState first. Write a couple of apps with it first. After you really feel the need, start learning about a state management approach.
emphases.from_slide_quote: WARNING: Learn the basics well before starting these approaches! Try it with setState first. Write a couple of apps with it first. After you really feel the need, start learning about a state management approach.

emphases.statement: The primary problems declarative UI aims to solve are the 'source of information' issue (data synchronization chaos) and the inherent difficulty humans have maintaining complex UI tree structures.
emphases.why: Understanding the problems that declarative UI was invented to solve provides the fundamental motivation for its existence and helps students appreciate the benefits of this paradigm. This foundational understanding is important for analytical questions.
emphases.evidence: The lecturer introduces 'A problem in UI software is source of information' and elaborates on the 'spaghetti code' it creates. This is directly linked to the complexity of managing UI trees, which is also explicitly stated as a difficulty for humans.
emphases.from_transcript_quote: So a problem in UI software is source of information... So this declarative UI is invented to get around this issue completely. And also user interfaces are usually like trees, okay?... As a human being, it's difficult for you to deal with trees, traverse trees, maintain trees, right?

seed_quiz: Compare and contrast imperative and declarative programming paradigms, providing examples of each in the context of UI development.
Explain the concept of UI as a tree structure. Why do declarative UI frameworks choose to recreate the entire UI tree on state changes, and why is this not inefficient?
Describe unidirectional data flow in declarative UIs. How are 'props' (or constructor arguments) and 'state' used, and how do components communicate events upwards?
What are the key advantages of using a declarative UI framework like Flutter or React Native for mobile application development?
When should a developer consider adopting an advanced state management solution (e.g., Redux, MobX) in a declarative UI project, according to the lecture's advice?

alignment.summary_chatty: This lecture provides a comprehensive introduction to Declarative UI, setting the stage for practical mobile app development. The instructor skillfully interweaves theoretical concepts with practical implications, often emphasizing the 'why' behind the declarative paradigm. While the slides outline key definitions and comparisons, the lecture elaborates with relatable analogies, real-world development scenarios, and crucial advice on best practices, particularly regarding Flutter's developer experience and UI recreation. There's strong alignment on core concepts like Imperative vs. Declarative, UI as a tree, and data flow, ensuring students get a robust understanding from both visual aids and verbal explanations. The emphasis on developer productivity and specific warnings for state management truly enhances the learning experience beyond just textbook definitions.

segments.text: vSE 380 – Mobile Application Development Lecture 2: Declarative UI Lecturer: Gazihan Alankuş All rights reserved, cannot be used in any educational institution other than Izmir University of Economics. Please look at the couple of slides for assignments (marked with TODO) A problem: source of information Declarative Programming • In • Flutter • React/React Native • Jetpack Compose (Android) • Swift UI (iOS) we create the user interface in a style called “declarative programming”. • The kind of programming that most of you are used to is called “imperative programming”. • Android SDK • iOS SDK • What is the difference? What exactly are they? A computer program vs. an electronic circuit Imperative vs. Declarative Imperative Declarative • Giving orders in detail, with explicit instructions • Describing how it should be • Do this, then do that, then do this other thing. • Programmer has full control. • Programmer has full responsibility. • The computer is dumb. • Here is how I would like it to look like. I don’t have control over how exactly you are doing it. • Programmer has limited control. • Programmer has limited responsibility. • The computer is smart. Imperative vs. Declarative Imperative Declarative • Java • C++ • Step-by-step programming • HTML • SQL • Designing a circuit-like system • Procedural languages • Functional languages Is it just the language? • No, it’s the style. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Mt7KnvnNGfk How are React Native and Flutter Declarative? • JavaScript and Dart are procedural languages. Shouldn’t it be imperative? • Answer: there are imperative parts of your code. The code that generates the UI is declarative. Declarative code from React Native and Flutter. Declarative code from React Native and Flutter. • Although the language is suitable for imperative code, the framework forces declarative code. • You create and tie UI elements together. • You do not modify or manage existing UI elements. • There is no such code in either: • txtField.text = “new text” • pnlMain.addChild(txtField) • Instead, there is such code: • new Panel(new TextField(“hello”)); UI Code • React Native • JSX • (short for JavaScript XML) • Similar to HTML • The description for the UI. Creates the UI. • Not actual code • Limited customization • No debugging • Flutter • Widget constructors • Named parameters • child for single child • children for multiple • Actual code! • Fully customizable • Fully debuggable UI is a tree • The UI is a tree structure • Creating a tree is easy • Modifying and maintaining a tree is difficult • This is why they chose declarative • We create and re-create UI elements. We do not manage existing elements. • What? How? • How do you make a text field say “one” first, and then say “two” after you press a button? Imperative Example • How do you make a text field say “one” first, and then say “two” after you press a button? Panel panel = new Panel(); TextField t = new TextField(); panel.add(t); t.text = “one”; Button b = new Button(); panel.add(b); b.addClickHandler(() { t.text = “two”; }); • THIS IS IMPERATIVE CODE. THIS MAKES SENSE. • This is not how React Native or Flutter do it. Declarative Example • How do you make a text field say “one” first, and then say “two” after you press a button? String currentValue = “one”; buildUi() { new Panel( new Text(currentValue), new Button( onClick: () { currentValue = “two”; rebuildUi(); } ) ) } • THIS IS DECLARATIVE CODE. THIS IS COUNTER-INTUITIVE • Why does React Native and Flutter use this pattern? Compare them. Panel panel = new Panel(); TextField t = new TextField(); panel.add(t); t.text = “one”; Button b = new Button(); panel.add(b); b.addClickHandler(() { String currentValue = “one”; buildUi() { new Panel( new Text(currentValue), new Button( onClick: () { currentValue = “two”; rebuildUi(); } ) t.text = “two”; }); ) } The difference • Imperative • Declarative • You create elements • You attach them together • You create elements • You attach them together • You keep a reference to elements • You do not keep a reference to elements • You are not responsible for maintaining elements • You do not give them orders • You create them and forget about them • You are responsible for mainaining them • You give them orders • You recreate them when something changes How can you keep recreating the UI? • Isn’t it inefficient? How can you keep recreating the UI? • Isn’t it inefficient? • NO! People smarter than us have implemented it carefully. • What you are creating is not the real UI element. It’s just a light description of them. • Very light data structure, system is ok with creating and destroying them quickly. • The framework finds the difference between two versions of the tree and modifies the UI elements whose description actually changed. • Carefully crafted framework to give you the ability to “just recreate the UI”. • DO NOT TRY TO OPTIMIZE ANYTHING ABOUT IT!!! • DO NOT CACHE UI ELEMENTS, ETC. KEEP RECREATING THEM. Planning and Creating a Declarative UI • How should you go about planning your UI? • https://reactjs.org/docs/thinking-in-react.html •FilterableProductTable • SearchBar • ProductTable • ProductCategoryRow • ProductRow How does data flow? https://livebook.manning.com/book/react-in-action/chapter-3/112 How does data flow? https://www.slideshare.net/pheadra4/react-46872849?from_action=save How does data flow? https://www.slideshare.net/pheadra4/react-46872849?from_action=save How does data flow? • Unidirectional data flow • Top to bottom • Props/constructor arguments (fixed) • Data being passed down when component is created (yellow). • State (changing) • Temporary data that changes with user input or other events • Triggers a rebuild of subtree. Data is passed down as new version of props. • Bottom to top • Events • Lower level component communicates up https://tkssharma.gitbook.io/react-training/day-01/react-js-3-principles/one-way-data-flow How does data flow? • Unidirectional data flow • Top to bottom • Props/constructor arguments (fixed) • Data being passed down when component is created (yellow). • State (changing) • Temporary data that changes with user input or other events • Triggers a rebuild of subtree. Data is passed down as new version of props. • Bottom to top • Events • Lower level component communicates up https://tkssharma.gitbook.io/react-training/day-01/react-js-3-principles/one-way-data-flow Other approaches • Passing data around can be cumbersome • All intermediate levels need to know about the data being passed to a leaf • Solution: state management • Redux • MobX • Provider • Etc. • WARNING: Learn the basics well before starting these approaches! • Try it with setState first. Write a couple of apps with it first. After you really feel the need, start learning about a state management approach. modules.loTitle: will be able to analyse the requirements of mobile applications
modules.oneLineGist: This LO was not covered in the specific lecture content provided. The lecture focused on UI design and development paradigms, not general mobile application requirements analysis.

modules.loTitle: will be able to design user interfaces and interactions for mobile applications
modules.oneLineGist: Learn the core principles of declarative UI design for mobile apps, contrasting it with imperative approaches and understanding data flow and state management.
coreIdeas: **Imperative vs. Declarative Programming**: Imperative gives detailed step-by-step instructions (e.g., Java, C++), while Declarative describes the desired outcome (e.g., HTML, SQL) and the system figures out how to achieve it.
**UI as a Tree Structure**: User interfaces are inherently hierarchical. While creating a UI tree is easy, modifying and maintaining it imperatively is complex and error-prone.

**Declarative UI Frameworks (Flutter, React Native)**: These frameworks (despite using procedural languages like Dart/JavaScript) force a declarative style. Instead of modifying existing UI elements, you describe and re-create the UI elements based on the current state.

**Efficiency of UI Re-creation**: Declarative frameworks efficiently re-create UI descriptions (light data structures) and then intelligently compare these descriptions to only update the actual UI elements that have changed, making it performant.
**Unidirectional Data Flow (Top-to-Bottom)**: Data flows from parent components to child components via two main mechanisms:
- **Props/Constructor Arguments**: Fixed data passed down when a component is created, used for configuring static aspects.
- **State**: Temporary, changing data internal to a component that triggers a rebuild of its subtree when updated.
**Bottom-to-Top Communication (Events)**: Lower-level components communicate changes or user interactions back up to parent components using events or callbacks.

**State Management Solutions**: For complex applications, direct prop-drilling can be cumbersome. Libraries like Redux, MobX, or Provider offer centralized state management solutions to simplify data sharing across distant components.
mustRemember: **Declarative UI focuses on 'what' (description), not 'how' (explicit steps).**
**In declarative UIs, you re-create UI descriptions, not modify existing elements directly.**
**'Props' are for fixed, parent-to-child data; 'State' is for changing, internal component data.**
**Declarative frameworks optimize UI updates; do NOT try to manually optimize by caching elements.**
**Unidirectional data flow ensures predictable UI behavior and easier debugging.**
modules.intuitiveExplanation: Imagine you're ordering a custom cake. Imperative is like giving the baker step-by-step instructions: 'First, mix flour and eggs, then bake for 30 minutes, then apply frosting.' You're in full control but also fully responsible. Declarative is like giving the baker a picture and saying, 'I want a cake that looks exactly like this, with 'Happy Birthday' written on it.' You describe the end result, and the smart baker (the framework) figures out the most efficient way to make it, even if you change your mind about the writing later. For UIs, this means you just tell the system what you want to see, and it redraws or updates the necessary parts efficiently, rather than you trying to find and tweak individual UI components.

examples.label: Imperative UI Code
examples.description: To change text in an imperative system: `TextField t = new TextField(); t.text = "one"; ... b.addClickHandler(() { t.text = "two"; });` (Directly modifying an existing UI object's property).

examples.label: Declarative UI Code
examples.description: To change text in a declarative system: `String currentValue = "one"; buildUi() { new Panel( new Text(currentValue), ... Button(onClick: () { currentValue = "two"; rebuildUi(); }) ); }` (Updating a variable and then triggering a re-render of the UI description).

examples.label: UI Planning/Decomposition
examples.description: Breaking down a complex UI like a 'FilterableProductTable' into smaller, reusable components: 'SearchBar', 'ProductTable', 'ProductCategoryRow', 'ProductRow'. Each component describes its own part of the UI based on its props and state.

typicalQuestions: Explain the fundamental difference between imperative and declarative programming paradigms, specifically in the context of UI development for mobile applications.
Describe how modern mobile UI frameworks like Flutter or React Native achieve declarative UI, even when using procedural languages like Dart or JavaScript.
How is data flow typically managed in a declarative UI? Differentiate between 'props' (or constructor arguments) and 'state' as sources of UI data.
You are tasked with designing a component that displays a user's profile picture and name. When would you use 'props' and when might you use 'state' for this component's data?
Why is it generally advised to 'not optimize' UI re-creation in declarative frameworks, and what mechanism makes this possible?
commonTraps: **Confusing UI element instances with their descriptions**: Thinking you're modifying a 'real' UI element directly in declarative code, rather than creating a new description.
**Trying to cache UI elements**: Attempting to reuse UI objects directly, which goes against the declarative philosophy and the framework's optimization strategy.
**Misunderstanding 'State' vs. 'Props'**: Using state for data that should be passed as props, or vice-versa, leading to inefficient updates or incorrect data flow.
**Assuming declarative re-creation is always slow**: Not trusting the framework's underlying optimization (virtual DOM/widget tree diffing) and attempting premature manual optimizations.
**Ignoring unidirectional data flow**: Trying to modify parent component data directly from a child component without using events/callbacks, leading to unpredictable behavior.

miniQuiz.question: What is the primary characteristic of imperative programming?
miniQuiz.answer: Giving explicit, detailed, step-by-step instructions on 'how' to achieve a result.
miniQuiz.why: This defines the core of imperative programming as discussed in the lecture.

miniQuiz.question: In declarative UI, do you modify existing UI elements or re-create their descriptions?
miniQuiz.answer: You re-create their descriptions (lightweight data structures).
miniQuiz.why: This is a fundamental concept of how declarative UI frameworks manage updates and state changes.

miniQuiz.question: What triggers a 'rebuild of subtree' in a declarative UI when data changes?
miniQuiz.why: State is the mechanism for internal, changing data that causes a component and its children to re-render.

miniQuiz.question: If a UI element's description needs to change, why is it not inefficient to recreate the entire description?
miniQuiz.answer: The framework creates lightweight descriptions, finds the differences (diffing algorithm), and only updates the actual UI elements that truly changed, making it efficient.
miniQuiz.why: This explains the optimization mechanism discussed by the lecturer, countering the common misconception of inefficiency.

# Overview of Mass Storage Structure
 Bulk of secondary storage for modern computers is
hard
disk
drives
(
HDDs
)
and
nonvolatile
memory
(
NVM
)
devices
 HDDs
spin platters of magnetically-coated material under moving
read-write heads
• Drives rotate at 60 to 250 times per second
• Transfer
rate
is rate at which data flow between drive and
computer
• Positioning
time
(
random-access
time
) is time to move disk arm
to desired cylinder (
seek
time
) and time for desired sector to
rotate under the disk head (
rotational
latency
)
• Head
crash
results from disk head making contact with the disk
surface  -- That
’
s bad
 Disks can be removable

# Hard Disk Drives
 Platters range from .85
”
to 14
”
(historically)
• Commonly 3.5
”
, 2.5
”
, and 1.8
”
 Range from 30GB to 3TB per drive
 Performance
• Transfer Rate – theoretical – 6
Gb/sec
• Effective Transfer Rate – real –
1Gb/sec
• Seek time from 3ms to 12ms –
9ms common for desktop drives
• Average seek time measured or
calculated based on 1/3 of tracks
• Latency based on spindle speed

1 / (RPM / 60) = 60 / RPM
• Average latency = ½ latency

# Hard Disk Performance
 Access
Latency
=
Average
access
time
= average seek time +
average latency
• For fastest disk 3ms + 2ms = 5ms
• For slow disk 9ms + 5.56ms = 14.56ms
 Average I/O time = average access time + (amount to transfer /
transfer rate) + controller overhead
 For example to transfer a 4KB block on a 7200 RPM disk with a 5ms
average seek time, 1Gb/sec transfer rate with a .1ms controller
overhead =
• 5ms + 4.17ms + 0.1ms + transfer time =
• Transfer time = 4KB / 1Gb/s * 8Gb / GB * 1GB / 1024
KB = 32 /
(1024
) = 0.031 ms
• Average I/O time for 4KB block = 9.27ms + .031ms = 9.301ms

# Nonvolatile Memory Devices
 If disk-drive like, then called
solid-state
disks
(
SSDs
)
 Other forms include
USB
drives
(thumb drive, flash drive), DRAM disk
replacements, surface-mounted on motherboards, and main storage in
devices like smartphones
 Can be more reliable than HDDs
 More expensive per MB
 Maybe have shorter life span – need careful management
 Less capacity
 But much faster
 Busses can be too slow -> connect directly to PCI for example
 No moving parts, so no seek time or rotational latency

--- Slide 10 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th

# Nonvolatile Memory Devices
 Have characteristics that present
challenges
 Read and written in “page” increments
(think sector) but can’t overwrite in
place
• Must first be erased, and erases
happen in larger ”block”
increments
• Can only be erased a limited
number of times before worn out –
~ 100,000
• Life span measured in
drive
writes
per
day
(
DWPD
)

A 1TB NAND drive with rating
of 5DWPD is expected to have
5TB per day written within
warrantee period without failing

# NAND Flash Controller Algorithms
 With no overwrite, pages end up with mix of valid and invalid data
 To track which logical blocks are valid, controller maintains
flash
translation
layer
(
FTL
) table
 Also implements
garbage
collection
to free invalid page space
 Allocates
overprovisioning
to provide working space for GC
 Each cell has lifespan, so
wear
leveling
needed to write equally to all
cells
NAND block with valid and invalid pages

# Volatile Memory
 DRAM frequently used as mass-storage device
• Not technically secondary storage because volatile, but can have
file systems, be used like very fast secondary storage
 RAM
drives
(with many names, including RAM disks) present as raw
block devices, commonly file system formatted
 Computers have buffering, caching via RAM, so why RAM drives?
• Caches / buffers allocated / managed by programmer, operating
system, hardware
• RAM drives under user control
• Found in all major operating systems

Linux
/dev/ram
, macOS
diskutil
to create them, Linux
/tmp
of file system type
tmpfs
 Used as high speed temporary storage
• Programs could share bulk date, quickly, by reading/writing to
RAM drive

# Disk Attachment
 Host-attached storage accessed through I/O ports talking to
I/O
busses
 Several busses available, including
advanced
technology
attachment
(
ATA
),
serial
ATA
(
SATA
),
eSATA
,
serial
attached
SCSI
(
SAS
),
universal
serial
bus
(
USB
), and
fibre
channel
(
FC
).
 Most common is SATA
 Because NVM much faster than HDD, new fast interface for NVM
called
NVM
express
(
NVMe
), connecting directly to PCI bus
 Data transfers on a bus carried out by special electronic processors
called
controllers
(or
host-bus
adapters
,
HBAs
)
• Host controller on the computer end of the bus, device controller on
device end
• Computer places command on host controller, using memory-
mapped I/O ports

Host controller sends messages to device controller

Data transferred via DMA between device and computer DRAM

--- Slide 15 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th
Edition
# Address Mapping
 Disk drives are addressed as large 1-dimensional arrays of
logical
blocks
, where the logical block is the smallest unit of transfer
• Low-level formatting creates
logical
blocks
on physical media
 The 1-dimensional array of logical blocks is mapped into the sectors of
the disk sequentially
• Sector 0 is the first sector of the first track on the outermost
cylinder
• Mapping proceeds in order through that track, then the rest of the
tracks in that cylinder, and then through the rest of the cylinders
from outermost to innermost
• Logical to physical address should be easy

Except for bad sectors

Non-constant # of sectors per track via constant angular
velocity

--- Slide 16 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th
Edition
# HDD Scheduling
 The operating system is responsible for using hardware efficiently —
for the disk drives, this means having a fast access time and disk
bandwidth
 Minimize seek time
 Seek time

seek distance
 Disk
bandwidth
is the total number of bytes transferred, divided by
the total time between the first request for service and the completion
of the last transfer

--- Slide 17 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th
Edition
# Disk Scheduling (Cont.)
 There are many sources of disk I/O request
• OS
• System processes
• Users processes
 I/O request includes input or output mode, disk address, memory
address, number of sectors to transfer
 OS maintains queue of requests, per disk or device
 Idle disk can immediately work on I/O request, busy disk means work
must queue
• Optimization algorithms only make sense when a queue exists
 In the past, operating system responsible for queue management, disk
drive head scheduling
• Now, built into the storage devices, controllers
• Just provide LBAs, handle sorting of requests

Some of the algorithms they use described next

th

 Note that drive controllers have small buffers and can manage a
queue of I/O requests (of varying
“
depth
”
)
 Several algorithms exist to schedule the servicing of disk I/O requests
 The analysis is true for one or many platters
 We illustrate scheduling algorithms with a request queue (0-199)

# SCAN
 The disk arm starts at one end of the disk, and moves toward the
other end, servicing requests until it gets to the other end of the disk,
where the head movement is reversed and servicing continues.
 SCAN
algorithm
Sometimes called the
elevator
algorithm
 Illustration shows total head movement of 208 cylinders
 But note that if requests are uniformly dense, largest density at other
end of disk and those wait the longest

# C-SCAN
 Provides a more uniform wait time than SCAN
 The head moves from one end of the disk to the other, servicing
requests as it goes
• When it reaches the other end, however, it immediately returns to
the beginning of the disk, without servicing any requests on the
return trip
 Treats the cylinders as a circular list that wraps around from the last
cylinder to the first one
 Total number of cylinders?

# Selecting a Disk-Scheduling Algorithm
 SSTF is common and has a natural appeal
 SCAN and C-SCAN perform better for systems that place a heavy load on the disk
• Less starvation, but still possible
 To avoid starvation Linux implements
## deadline
scheduler
• Maintains separate read and write queues, gives read priority

Because processes more likely to block on read than write
• Implements four queues: 2 x read and 2 x write

1 read and 1 write queue sorted in LBA order, essentially implementing
C-SCAN

1 read and 1 write queue sorted in FCFS order

All I/O requests sent in batch sorted in that queue’s order

After each batch, checks if any requests in FCFS older than configured
age (default 500ms)
–
If so, LBA queue containing that request is selected for next batch of
I/O
 In RHEL 7 also
NOOP
and
completely
fair
queueing
scheduler (
CFQ
) also
available, defaults vary by storage device

th

# NVM Scheduling
 No disk heads or rotational latency but still room for optimization
 In RHEL 7
NOOP
(no scheduling) is used but adjacent LBA requests
are combined
• NVM best at random I/O, HDD at sequential
• Throughput can be similar
• Input/Output operations per second
(
IOPS
) much higher with
NVM (hundreds of thousands vs hundreds)
• But
write
amplification
(one write, causing garbage collection and
many read/writes) can decrease the performance advantage

# Error Detection and Correction
 Fundamental aspect of many parts of computing (memory, networking,
storage)
 Error
detection
determines if there a problem has occurred (for
example a bit flipping)
• If detected, can halt the operation
• Detection frequently done via parity bit
 Parity one form of
checksum
– uses modular arithmetic to compute,
store, compare values of fixed-length words
• Another error-detection method common in networking is
cyclic
redundancy
check
(
CRC
) which uses hash function to detect
multiple-bit errors
 Error-correction
code
(
ECC
) not only detects, but can correct some
errors
• Soft errors correctable, hard errors detected but not corrected

, or
physical
formatting

)

formatting

“

”

# Cloud Storage
 Similar to NAS, provides access to storage across a network
• Unlike NAS, accessed over the Internet or a WAN to remote
data center
 NAS presented as just another file system, while cloud storage is
API based, with programs using the APIs to provide access
• Examples include Dropbox, Amazon S3, Microsoft OneDrive,
Apple iCloud
• Use APIs because of latency and failure scenarios (NAS
protocols wouldn’t work well)

 Avoids the NAS drawback of using network bandwidth

• Ports to connect hosts to array
• Memory, controlling software (sometimes NVRAM, etc)
• A few to thousands of disks
• RAID, hot spares, hot swap (discussed later)
• Shared storage -> more efficiency
• Features found in some file systems

etc

 Regardless of where RAID implemented, other useful features can be
added

professorEmphases.statement: Hard disks are still relevant due to their use in data centers, especially with the growth of AI.
professorEmphases.why: Despite the increasing popularity of SSDs, understanding HDDs is essential for understanding the landscape of storage solutions and their applications in large-scale environments.
professorEmphases.evidence: The professor explicitly mentions that the initial plan was to discard the topic of hard disks, but they remained relevant because of data centers.
professorEmphases.from_transcript_quote: the authors also were planning to just discard the whole thing because they considered out of date up until this data center usage, especially with AI growing drastically.

professorEmphases.statement: Transfer rate is an important parameter because the speed of reading bits by the head has to match the speed of the transfer rate.
professorEmphases.why: Mismatch leads to buffering delays, impacting performance.
professorEmphases.evidence: Lecturer emphasizes the importance of matching read speeds and transfer rates. The slides mention transfer rate.
professorEmphases.from_transcript_quote: the speed of reading bits by head has to match the speed of transfer rate otherwise there will be an accumulation of real data to be buffered and we don't have much time for buffering that would further make us lose some time.
professorEmphases.from_slide_quote: Transfer rate is rate at which data flow between drive and computer

professorEmphases.statement: Positional delay, or seek time, is the dominant factor affecting hard disk performance.
professorEmphases.why: Understanding and minimizing seek time is critical for optimizing I/O operations.
professorEmphases.evidence: Lecturer repeatedly mentions positional delay as the primary bottleneck. Slides show access latency formulas.
professorEmphases.from_transcript_quote: the positional delay we will talk about will be the one dominating this by your time.
professorEmphases.from_slide_quote: Seek time from 3ms to 12ms – 9ms common for desktop drives

professorEmphases.statement: The concept of cylinders is introduced to minimize the movement of the arm assembly.
professorEmphases.why: By filling a cylinder before moving to another, we can reduce the number of positional delays.
professorEmphases.evidence: The lecturer goes into detail about cylinders, equidistant tracks and reading or writing as much data as possible.
professorEmphases.from_transcript_quote: Because when this one moves we would like to read or write as much data as possible because we don't want to make this movement very, very frequent. So the mechanism, the cylinder mechanism is the one where we have the tracks of the same radius from the spinal.

professorEmphases.statement: Access time consists of positional delay, rotational delay, transfer delay, and controller overhead.
professorEmphases.why: Breaking down access time into these components allows for a better understanding of each contributing factor.
professorEmphases.evidence: Lecturer explicitly outlines all these components. Slides contain formula.
professorEmphases.from_transcript_quote: position of delay plus rotational delay plus transfer delay plus control over head or control delay for components of this.
professorEmphases.from_slide_quote: Average I/O time = average access time + (amount to transfer / transfer rate) + controller overhead

professorEmphases.statement: Error detection and correction is fundamental to storage.
professorEmphases.why: Errors can occur. Need to know how to catch and fix them.
professorEmphases.evidence: Lecture highlights this. Slide has a section devoted to it.
professorEmphases.from_transcript_quote: So that is the computation we carried out the dirty work just to do it
professorEmphases.from_slide_quote: Error detection determines if there a problem has occurred (for example a bit flipping)

modules.title: HDD Fundamentals and Performance
modules.goal: Understand the physical structure of hard disk drives and the factors affecting their performance.

lessons.title: Physical Structure of HDDs
lessons.objective: Identify the key components of a hard disk drive and describe their function.

activities.prompt: Read the textbook chapter on HDD structure, paying attention to platters, read-write heads, and the actuator arm.
activities.expected_outcome: Understanding of HDD components and their spatial relationships.

activities.prompt: Watch a video demonstrating the internal workings of an HDD. Focus on how data is read and written.
activities.expected_outcome: Visual understanding of read/write process.

mini_quiz: What are the main components of a hard disk drive?
What is the function of the read-write head?
What happens during a head crash?

lessons.title: HDD Performance Metrics
lessons.objective: Calculate access time and understand the impact of seek time and rotational latency.

activities.prompt: Read the textbook section on HDD performance, focusing on seek time, rotational latency, and transfer rate.
activities.expected_outcome: Knowledge of HDD performance parameters.

activities.prompt: Solve practice problems involving access time calculation. Vary seek time and rotational latency values.
activities.expected_outcome: Ability to calculate access time accurately.

mini_quiz: What is the difference between seek time and rotational latency?
How does RPM affect latency?
Define access time and its components.

modules.title: Solid State Drives and Memory Management
modules.goal: Understand the operation of Solid State Drives, their advantages, limitations and memory management techniques

lessons.title: SSD Architecture and Operation
lessons.objective: Describe the internal architecture of SSDs and explain how they store and retrieve data.

activities.prompt: Read the assigned material on SSD architecture, focusing on NAND flash memory, blocks, and pages.
activities.expected_outcome: Comprehension of SSD internal structure.

activities.prompt: Watch a video illustrating how data is written and erased in SSDs.
activities.expected_outcome: Visual understanding of SSD write/erase cycles.

mini_quiz: What is NAND flash memory?
What are blocks and pages in SSDs?
Why can't SSDs overwrite data in place?

lessons.title: Memory Management in SSDs
lessons.objective: Explain the concepts of wear leveling, garbage collection, and overprovisioning in SSDs.

activities.prompt: Read about wear leveling, garbage collection, and overprovisioning in SSDs. Understand their purpose and implementation.
activities.expected_outcome: Knowledge of SSD memory management techniques.

activities.prompt: Explain in your own words how wear leveling prevents premature SSD failure.
activities.expected_outcome: Ability to articulate the benefits of wear leveling.

mini_quiz: What is wear leveling and why is it necessary?
Explain the purpose of garbage collection in SSDs.
What is overprovisioning and how does it benefit SSD performance?

modules.title: Disk Scheduling and Storage Management
modules.goal: Evaluate different disk scheduling algorithms and understand storage device management techniques.

lessons.title: Disk Scheduling Algorithms
lessons.objective: Compare and contrast FCFS, SCAN, C-SCAN, and SSTF disk scheduling algorithms.

activities.prompt: Read the textbook chapter on disk scheduling algorithms. Pay attention to the advantages and disadvantages of each algorithm.
activities.expected_outcome: Knowledge of various disk scheduling algorithms.

activities.prompt: Work through examples demonstrating how each algorithm schedules disk requests.
activities.expected_outcome: Ability to apply disk scheduling algorithms to example scenarios.

mini_quiz: Describe the FCFS disk scheduling algorithm.
How does SCAN improve upon FCFS?
What is the main advantage of C-SCAN over SCAN?
What is SSTF and what are its potential drawbacks?

lessons.title: Storage Device Management Techniques
lessons.objective: Describe logical formatting and understand disk partitioning.

activities.prompt: Read the material about physical and logical formatting.
activities.expected_outcome: Comprehension of format operations.

activities.prompt: Create a disk partition scheme for a given scenario (e.g., dual-booting operating systems).
activities.expected_outcome: Practical understanding of disk partitioning.

mini_quiz: What is the purpose of physical formatting?
What is the purpose of logical formatting?
What is a disk partition and why is it used?

alignment.summary_chatty: The lecture and slides cover the chapter systematically, starting with hard disk drives (HDDs), then moving to solid state drives (SSDs) and other NVM devices, and finally discussing disk scheduling and management. The lecturer provides a detailed explanation of HDD components and performance metrics, including access time, seek time, and rotational latency. He also performs calculations to illustrate these concepts. The slides supplement the lecture with diagrams, formulas, and bullet points, providing a visual and structured overview of the material. The lecturer uses more anecdotes and context than the slides.

Error-correction code (ECC) not only detects, but can correct some errors
Disk Attachment
Host-attached storage accessed through I/O ports talking to I/O busses
Logical blocks

learningOutcomes: Measure the efficiency in using the resources of a computer.
Evaluate the convenience in using the resources of a computer.
Explain the mechanisms developed between different components for the purpose of overall efficient use of a computer.
Explain the techniques in developing systems programs.
Classify the programs as systems and user programs.

cheatSheet.title: CE 323: Mass Storage Systems Cheat Sheet

sections.heading: HDD Structure & Components
bullets: Platters rotate rapidly (60-250 times/second).
Arm assembly with magnetic heads reads/writes data.
Heads don't physically contact the platter (disk crash if they do).
Cylinders: Equidistant tracks minimize arm movement.
Transfer rate is critical for matching head read speed.

sections.heading: HDD Performance Factors
bullets: Access time = Positional + Rotational + Transfer Delay + Controller Overhead.
Positional delay (seek time) dominates access time.
Rotational delay: Time for sector to rotate under the head.
Transfer delay: Time to transfer the data.
Cylinders minimize seek time.

sections.heading: Key HDD Parameters
bullets: Diameter: common is 3.5 inches.
Transfer Rate (theoretical): ~6 Gb/sec.
Transfer Rate (real): ~1 Gb/sec.
Seek time: 3-12 ms (9ms typical).
Latency: Based on spindle speed (RPM).

sections.heading: SSD (NVM) Characteristics
bullets: No moving parts: faster access, no seek/rotational latency.
Read/written in 'pages,' erased in 'blocks'.
Erase limited number of times (wear).
Flash Translation Layer (FTL) maps logical blocks.
Garbage collection reclaims invalid page space.

sections.heading: Access Time Calculation
bullets: Average access time = Seek time + Latency.
Average I/O time includes access time, transfer time, and controller overhead.
Transfer Time = Amount to transfer / Transfer rate
Controller Overhead: Delay introduced by controller.

sections.heading: Storage Attachment Interfaces
bullets: Common interfaces: SATA, eSATA, SAS, USB, Fibre Channel (FC).
NVMe: Fast interface for NVM, connects directly to PCI.
Controllers (HBAs) manage data transfers on the bus.
Host controller on computer side, device controller on device side.

sections.heading: Disk Scheduling
bullets: Optimize disk access by minimizing seek time and maximizing bandwidth.
OS maintains queue of I/O requests.
Modern drives have built-in scheduling, using LBAs.
Goal: Efficient use of hardware.
Disk bandwidth: Total bytes transferred / total time

formulas: Average access time = Seek time + Latency
Average I/O time = average access time + (amount to transfer / transfer rate) + controller overhead
Latency = 1 / (RPM / 60) (full rotation), Average latency = 1/2 of that
Transfer Time = Amount to transfer / Transfer rate
pitfalls: Forgetting that seek time is the dominant factor in HDD latency.
Ignoring controller overhead in I/O time calculations.
Assuming theoretical transfer rates are achievable in practice.
Overlooking the limited erase cycles of SSDs (wear leveling importance).
Not understanding cylinder concept for HDD performance.
Misunderstanding the difference between positional and rotational delay.
Failing to match the data reading and transfer speeds between head and computer.

quickQuiz.q: What are the main components of HDD access time?
quickQuiz.a: Positional delay (seek time), rotational delay, transfer delay, and controller overhead.

quickQuiz.q: Why are cylinders important in HDDs?
quickQuiz.a: Minimize arm movement, read/write more data before moving.

quickQuiz.q: What is the main advantage of SSDs over HDDs?
quickQuiz.a: Faster access due to no moving parts (no seek/rotational latency).

quickQuiz.q: What is wear leveling and why is it needed?
quickQuiz.a: Writing data equally to all SSD cells, extends lifespan.

quickQuiz.q: What is the role of the Flash Translation Layer (FTL)?
quickQuiz.a: Tracks valid logical blocks in SSDs to handle no overwrite

modules.loTitle: Measure the efficiency in using the resources of a computer.
modules.oneLineGist: Hard disk drive (HDD) efficiency is measured by access time, transfer rate, and factors like seek time and rotational latency. Solid state drives (SSDs) have different efficiency metrics related to read/write cycles and memory management.
coreIdeas: Access time (seek time + rotational latency) impacts HDD efficiency.
Transfer rate between drive and computer is a key performance metric.
Rotational latency depends on the disk's RPM.
SSD efficiency is affected by erase cycles, wear leveling, and garbage collection.
Efficiency considerations differ between HDDs and SSDs due to their distinct technologies.
Data center usage and AI growth are driving the relevance of HDDs due to their cost-effectiveness for high-capacity storage.
mustRemember: Access time components: seek time and rotational latency.
Transfer rate definition: speed of data flow between drive and computer.
RPM's impact on rotational latency: higher RPM, lower latency.
SSD wear leveling: distributes writes to extend lifespan.
HDDs still relevant in data centers because of their large capacities.
modules.intuitiveExplanation: Think of HDD efficiency like ordering food at a restaurant. Seek time is how long it takes the waiter to find your table, rotational latency is how long you wait for the chef to finish preparing that specific dish, and transfer rate is how quickly you can eat it!  For SSDs, imagine a whiteboard you can only erase and rewrite a limited number of times; wear leveling tries to spread the writing evenly to make the board last longer. Data centers use HDDs a lot because it's like getting a huge buffet for a low price, even if it takes a bit longer to get each dish.

examples.label: Access Time Calculation
examples.description: Calculate the access time for a disk with a 5ms seek time and 3ms rotational latency. Access time = 5ms + 3ms = 8ms.

examples.label: Transfer Rate Impact
examples.description: A faster transfer rate means data can be read or written more quickly, improving overall system performance.

typicalQuestions: Explain how seek time and rotational latency affect hard disk performance.
How does RPM influence the efficiency of a hard disk drive?
Compare the factors that determine the efficiency of HDDs and SSDs.
Why are HDDs still relevant despite the rise of SSDs in personal computers?
commonTraps: Confusing seek time and rotational latency.
Ignoring the impact of RPM on latency calculations.
Assuming SSDs are always more efficient than HDDs in all scenarios (capacity vs. speed tradeoffs).
Forgetting the role of wear leveling in SSD lifespan.

miniQuiz.question: What are the two main components of access time in an HDD?
miniQuiz.why: Access time is the sum of seek time (arm movement) and rotational latency (waiting for the sector).

miniQuiz.question: How does a higher RPM generally affect rotational latency?
miniQuiz.why: Higher RPM means faster rotation, reducing the time to reach the desired sector.

miniQuiz.question: What is wear leveling and why is it used in SSDs?
miniQuiz.answer: Writing data evenly across memory cells to extend lifespan.
miniQuiz.why: SSDs have limited write cycles per cell; wear leveling prevents premature failure.

modules.loTitle: Evaluate the convenience in using the resources of a computer.
modules.oneLineGist: Convenience in using mass storage resources involves factors like access time, ease of storage device management (e.g., partitioning), and the presence of operating system services for efficient I/O.
coreIdeas: Faster access times in SSDs provide more convenient user experience compared to HDDs.
Storage device management techniques, such as partitioning, improve organization and convenience.
Operating system services for I/O, including scheduling algorithms, enhance usability.
Swap space management contributes to overall system responsiveness and convenience when physical memory is limited.
The physical structure of storage devices directly affects access speed and therefore, convenience.
mustRemember: SSD access times are significantly lower than HDD access times.
Disk partitioning allows for better organization and management of storage space.
I/O scheduling algorithms optimize disk access, improving responsiveness.
Swap space is used when RAM is full to allow running more programmes, at a cost to overall performance.
modules.intuitiveExplanation: Think about convenience like this: an SSD is like grabbing a snack from a shelf right next to you, quick and easy. An HDD is like having to walk to the store to get that same snack - it takes longer. Disk partitioning is like organizing your closet so you know exactly where everything is. Good I/O scheduling is like having someone manage a queue so everyone gets served in a reasonable time. Swap space is like using a small desk when the big one is full, it's convenient but slows you down a bit.

examples.label: SSD vs. HDD Boot Time
examples.description: SSDs offer much faster boot times, making them more convenient for frequently used computers.

examples.label: Partitioning for Dual-Boot
examples.description: Partitioning a hard drive to install multiple operating systems allows users to conveniently switch between them.

typicalQuestions: How do SSDs improve the convenience of using computer resources compared to HDDs?
Explain how disk partitioning enhances storage device management and usability.
Describe the role of I/O scheduling algorithms in improving system responsiveness.
How does swap space contribute to the convenience of running multiple applications simultaneously?
commonTraps: Assuming convenience is solely determined by access speed.
Ignoring the impact of storage device management on usability.
Overlooking the role of operating system services in optimizing I/O.
Underestimating the impact of disk fragmentation on performance over time.

miniQuiz.question: What is a key advantage of SSDs regarding user convenience?
miniQuiz.answer: Faster access times (boot times, application loading).
miniQuiz.why: SSDs offer significantly lower latency, resulting in a snappier user experience.

miniQuiz.question: What does disk partitioning enable users to do?
miniQuiz.answer: Organize storage space, install multiple OSs, etc.
miniQuiz.why: Partitioning provides better management and isolation of data on a storage device.

miniQuiz.question: How do I/O scheduling algorithms improve user experience?
miniQuiz.answer: Optimize disk access and improve responsiveness.
miniQuiz.why: Efficient scheduling minimizes waiting times for I/O requests.

modules.loTitle: Explain the mechanisms developed between different components for the purpose of overall efficient use of a computer.
modules.oneLineGist: Mechanisms for efficient computer use include the physical structure of HDDs (cylinders), transfer rate matching, and SSD management techniques (wear leveling, garbage collection).
coreIdeas: Cylinder organization in HDDs optimizes data access by grouping equidistant tracks.
Matching transfer rates between the drive and computer prevents data accumulation and buffering delays.
SSD wear leveling distributes write operations to prolong lifespan.
Garbage collection in SSDs frees up invalid page space.
Flash translation layer (FTL) in SSDs tracks valid logical blocks.
RAID structures combine multiple physical drives for performance and reliability
mustRemember: Cylinders: tracks with the same radius on different platters.
Importance of matching transfer rate: avoids buffering delays.
Wear leveling: distributes writes evenly across SSD cells.
Garbage collection: reclaims invalid pages in SSDs.
RAID: Redundant Array of Independent Disks
HDDs are still relevant in data centers because of their large capacities and costs savings.
modules.intuitiveExplanation: Think of cylinders in HDDs as organizing your books on shelves so that you can grab many related books without moving much. Matching transfer rates is like having the right sized pipe for water to flow without bottlenecks. SSD wear leveling is like rotating the tires on your car so they wear out evenly. Garbage collection is like cleaning your room to free up space.

examples.label: Cylinder Organization
examples.description: Data is stored sequentially by cylinder to minimize arm movement in HDDs.

examples.label: Transfer Rate Bottleneck
examples.description: If the transfer rate between the drive and computer doesn't match, data accumulates requiring buffering and slowing the whole operation down.

examples.label: RAID 0 (Striping)
examples.description: Data is split across multiple disks to increase read and write speeds, but with no redundancy.

typicalQuestions: Explain the purpose of organizing tracks into cylinders in hard disk drives.
Why is it important to match the transfer rate between the drive and the computer?
Describe how wear leveling improves the lifespan of solid-state drives.
What is the purpose of garbage collection in SSDs?
How does RAID structure enhance computer performance and reliability?
Why are hard disks still relevant for mass storage in data centers?
commonTraps: Misunderstanding the spatial relationship of tracks within a cylinder.
Ignoring the impact of mismatched transfer rates on I/O performance.
Confusing wear leveling with garbage collection in SSDs.
Failing to grasp the different RAID levels and their tradeoffs.
Ignoring the cost/benefit tradeoff of SSD vs HDD

miniQuiz.question: What is a cylinder in a hard disk drive?
miniQuiz.answer: Tracks with the same radius on different platters.
miniQuiz.why: Cylinders minimize arm movement, improving access time.

miniQuiz.question: Why is matching transfer rates important?
miniQuiz.answer: Avoids buffering delays and data accumulation.
miniQuiz.why: Ensures smooth data flow without bottlenecks.

miniQuiz.question: What does wear leveling accomplish in SSDs?
miniQuiz.answer: Distributes writes evenly to prolong lifespan.
miniQuiz.why: Prevents premature failure due to limited write cycles.

 Describe the physical structure of secondary storage devices and
the effect of a device’s structure on its uses
 Explain the performance characteristics of mass-storage devices
 Evaluate I/O scheduling algorithms
 Discuss operating-system services provided for mass storage,

# Overview of Mass Storage Structure
 Bulk of secondary storage for modern computers is
hard
disk
drives
(
HDDs
)
and
nonvolatile
memory
(
NVM
)
devices
 HDDs
spin platters of magnetically-coated material under moving
read-write heads
• Drives rotate at 60 to 250 times per second
• Transfer
rate
is rate at which data flow between drive and
computer
• Positioning
time
(
random-access
time
) is time to move disk arm
to desired cylinder (
seek
time
) and time for desired sector to
rotate under the disk head (
rotational
latency
)
• Head
crash
results from disk head making contact with the disk
surface  -- That
’
s bad
 Disks can be removable

# Hard Disk Drives
 Platters range from .85
”
to 14
”
(historically)
• Commonly 3.5
”
, 2.5
”
, and 1.8
”
 Range from 30GB to 3TB per drive
 Performance
• Transfer Rate – theoretical – 6
Gb/sec
• Effective Transfer Rate – real –
1Gb/sec
• Seek time from 3ms to 12ms –
9ms common for desktop drives
• Average seek time measured or
calculated based on 1/3 of tracks
• Latency based on spindle speed

1 / (RPM / 60) = 60 / RPM
• Average latency = ½ latency

# Hard Disk Performance
 Access
Latency
=
Average
access
time
= average seek time +
average latency
• For fastest disk 3ms + 2ms = 5ms
• For slow disk 9ms + 5.56ms = 14.56ms
 Average I/O time = average access time + (amount to transfer /
transfer rate) + controller overhead
 For example to transfer a 4KB block on a 7200 RPM disk with a 5ms
average seek time, 1Gb/sec transfer rate with a .1ms controller
overhead =
• 5ms + 4.17ms + 0.1ms + transfer time =
• Transfer time = 4KB / 1Gb/s * 8Gb / GB * 1GB / 1024
KB = 32 /
(1024
) = 0.031 ms
• Average I/O time for 4KB block = 9.27ms + .031ms = 9.301ms

# Nonvolatile Memory Devices
 If disk-drive like, then called
solid-state
disks
(
SSDs
)
 Other forms include
USB
drives
(thumb drive, flash drive), DRAM disk
replacements, surface-mounted on motherboards, and main storage in
devices like smartphones
 Can be more reliable than HDDs
 More expensive per MB
 Maybe have shorter life span – need careful management
 Less capacity
 But much faster
 Busses can be too slow -> connect directly to PCI for example
 No moving parts, so no seek time or rotational latency

--- Slide 10 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th
Edition
# Nonvolatile Memory Devices
 Have characteristics that present
challenges
 Read and written in “page” increments
(think sector) but can’t overwrite in
place
• Must first be erased, and erases
happen in larger ”block”
increments
• Can only be erased a limited
number of times before worn out –
~ 100,000
• Life span measured in
drive
writes
per
day
(
DWPD
)

A 1TB NAND drive with rating
of 5DWPD is expected to have
5TB per day written within
warrantee period without failing

# NAND Flash Controller Algorithms
 With no overwrite, pages end up with mix of valid and invalid data
 To track which logical blocks are valid, controller maintains
flash
translation
layer
(
FTL
) table
 Also implements
garbage
collection
to free invalid page space
 Allocates
overprovisioning
to provide working space for GC
 Each cell has lifespan, so
wear
leveling
needed to write equally to all
cells
NAND block with valid and invalid pages

# Volatile Memory
 DRAM frequently used as mass-storage device
• Not technically secondary storage because volatile, but can have
file systems, be used like very fast secondary storage
 RAM
drives
(with many names, including RAM disks) present as raw
block devices, commonly file system formatted
 Computers have buffering, caching via RAM, so why RAM drives?
• Caches / buffers allocated / managed by programmer, operating
system, hardware
• RAM drives under user control
• Found in all major operating systems

Linux
/dev/ram
, macOS
diskutil
to create them, Linux
/tmp
of file system type
tmpfs
 Used as high speed temporary storage
• Programs could share bulk date, quickly, by reading/writing to
RAM drive

# Disk Attachment
 Host-attached storage accessed through I/O ports talking to
I/O
busses
 Several busses available, including
advanced
technology
attachment
(
ATA
),
serial
ATA
(
SATA
),
eSATA
,
serial
attached
SCSI
(
SAS
),
universal
serial
bus
(
USB
), and
fibre
channel
(
FC
).
 Most common is SATA
 Because NVM much faster than HDD, new fast interface for NVM
called
NVM
express
(
NVMe
), connecting directly to PCI bus
 Data transfers on a bus carried out by special electronic processors
called
controllers
(or
host-bus
adapters
,
HBAs
)
• Host controller on the computer end of the bus, device controller on
device end
• Computer places command on host controller, using memory-
mapped I/O ports

Host controller sends messages to device controller

Data transferred via DMA between device and computer DRAM

--- Slide 15 (Extracted) ---
Operating System Concepts – 10
th
